clinical_history: ECG recorded from a middle-aged man. What is the diagnosis?
diagnosis: The ECG shows sinus rhythm with a classical WPW pre-excitation pattern. Note the triad of a: short PR interval (100 ms), wide QRS (140 ms), and delta waves (slurring of the QRS onset). The QRS morphology is indicative of a posterior (probably postero-septal) bypass tract. This pattern mimics an inferior (-posterior) infarct, and also may mistaken for RVH and/or RBBB. The ST-T changes are secondary to the altered depolarization pattern, which is opposite in polarity to the major QRS vector in the frontal and horizontal planes. The patient presented with a narrow complex tachycardia consistent with a reentrant circuit (down the AV node and back up the bypass tract in a retrograde direction). This type of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT) is referred to as orthodromic atrioventricular reentrant tachycardia (AVRT).
